title: Chapter 7: The Boardroom
content: The fluorescent lights hum at a frequency designed to be unnoticeable. I notice it. I notice everything now — the way the hum dips when someone turns on a lamp three desks over, the way the HVAC clicks exactly every forty-seven seconds, the way stale coffee smells different at 6 a.m. than it does at noon. This morning it smells like burnt plastic and regret. I'm on my third cup. My third cup since four a.m., which was when I gave up on sleep entirely, because sleep meant dreams, and dreams meant Paris, and Paris meant—
The prospectus hits my desk like a gunshot.
Two hundred pages, bound, heavy. The cover is stark: MERIDIAN CORP ACQUISITION — CONFIDENTIAL. I stare at it. Then I stare up at the man who dropped it there.

Julian Vance doesn't wait for my reaction. He's already two steps past my desk, moving down the aisle between the cubicles with that particular stride — purposeful, unhurried, the walk of a man who has never once in his life been asked to repeat himself.
"Presentation. Thursday." He says it over his shoulder, not breaking stride. "You're leading the financial modeling."
And then he's gone. Through the glass walls of the senior partners' corridor, swallowed by the machine of Sterling Capital.
I look back at the prospectus. My name isn't on it. There's no note, no delegation memo, no CC line with my email. Just the weight of two hundred pages and the implicit demand that I carry them.

Leading financial modeling. On a live M&A deal. First-year analysts don't lead financial modeling on live deals. First-year analysts build the models, stress-test the models, format the models until the font is the correct shade of navy blue — but they don't stand in front of senior leadership and present them.
This is either a promotion or a firing with extra steps.

I flip the cover open. The executive summary is dense, clinical, full of the language of acquisition: synergies, cost optimization, strategic alignment. Meridian Corp. A mid-cap pharmaceutical company with a European subsidiary that accounts for roughly a third of their revenue stream. The valuation models will need to account for currency exposure, regulatory risk, and at least three different carry-forward scenarios.
Forty-eight hours.
I pull up Excel. I start to read.
The next two days collapse into a single breathless blur of cells and formulas.

I don't go home. I shower in the gym on the fourteenth floor — two minutes, scalding water, no shampoo because I forgot to bring any. I eat whatever's in the communal kitchen: stale granola, a banana that's transitioned from ripe to philosophical, coffee that tastes like it was brewed during the previous administration.
But the numbers. The numbers flow.

I've always been good at this — the pattern recognition, the way disparate data points resolve into shape if you stare long enough. But this is different. This is better. The models build themselves in my head before I type a single formula, like the architecture was already there, waiting. I see connections between Meridian's R&D spend and their European margin compression that the prospectus doesn't flag. I find a correlation in their patent filings that suggests a pipeline product nobody's talking about. The DCF valuation practically writes itself.
I'm good at this. Actually good. Not performing, not overcompensating, not proving — just good.
Except.
Except I keep slipping.

It starts on the first night. I'm deep in the European revenue breakdown when I hear it — accordion music, faint and French, drifting through the office like a ghost. I look up. Nobody. The floor is empty except for the cleaning staff. The music is gone. I put my headphones on, crank white noise, and keep working.

Then it's the salt air. I'm reviewing currency hedge projections, mouth full of cold takeout pad thai, and suddenly I taste the Atlantic. Brine and kelp and that particular mineral sweetness of seawater, so vivid I turn around expecting to see a window, expecting to see the coast. There's just the wall. Beige. Fluorescent-lit. The taste fades.
By the second night, I'm tasting salt air every time I close my eyes.

I tell myself it's sleep deprivation. I tell myself the mind manufactures what the body craves, and my body craves Paris — the rain, the gallery, the man in the doorway with gray eyes who wasn't Julian but was. I tell myself this is normal. Occupational hazard of running on three hours of broken sleep with an app on my phone that claims to be mapping my lives across parallel timelines.
I tell myself this, and I keep building the model, and the model is beautiful.
Thursday arrives like a verdict.

The boardroom is on the forty-second floor, all glass and walnut and the kind of chair that costs more than my rent. The leather is cold when I sit down, and the cold seeps through my blouse, and I am suddenly, acutely aware that I am the youngest person in this room by at least a decade.

Sterling Capital's senior leadership files in like a jury. The CFO, the COO, three managing directors, two VPs, and — at the head of the table — Julian Vance. He doesn't look at me. He's reading something on his tablet, his pen moving in small, precise notations against the margin. Gray suit, white shirt, no tie. The collar is open exactly one button more than professional. It's the kind of detail you only notice if you're looking too hard.
I'm looking too hard.
"Whenever you're ready, Chen." He doesn't look up.
I stand. The click of my heels is too loud on the hardwood. I pull up the presentation on the screen and I open my mouth and—
I am on fire.

I don't know how else to describe it. The first slide is the executive summary, and I'm supposed to narrate it, but I've already moved past it because everyone in this room can read. "The headline numbers are in the deck," I say. "What matters is what's underneath." I click to the DCF. I walk them through the terminal growth assumption, the weighted cost of capital, the sensitivity analysis that shows a corridor of fair value between forty-two and fifty-one per share. I answer the COO's question before he finishes asking it — "You're wondering about the leverage implications of the earnout structure, and yes, I've modeled a downside scenario at twelve percent carry." He blinks. Nods.

A VP asks about the tax asset amortization. I've got it. I've got the exact schedule memorized, the stepped-up basis, the Section 338 implications. The numbers come out of me like water — clean, pressurized, unhesitating.

The CFO leans back in his chair. He actually nods. The CFO of Sterling Capital nods at me, Avery Chen, first-year analyst, who is currently standing in front of eleven people who collectively control more money than the GDP of several small nations, and I am not drowning. I am surfing.

Twelve minutes of this. Twelve minutes where I am the smartest person in the room, and I know it, and they know it, and the air around me is electric with the surprising fact of my competence. Julian's expression hasn't changed. His face is carved from the same indifferent marble it always is — the high cheekbones, the jaw like a paper weight, the eyes that dissect more than observe. But his pen has stopped moving. His pen, which has been scribbling notes since the moment I started, is now still, resting against the tablet, and his hand is just — suspended.
That's the tell. I've learned his tells the way you learn a language by immersion. Pen moving: engaged, critical, evaluating. Pen still: surprised. Julian Vance does not get surprised.
For twelve minutes, I surprise him.
Then it all goes wrong.

Hendricks — one of the managing directors, silver-haired, old-money, the kind of man who says "Europe" like it's a diagnosis — leans forward. "What about risk exposure in the European market? Meridian's French subsidiary accounts for, what, thirty-two percent of revenue? How are you modeling the regulatory environment?"
I have the answer. It's in my model. I built the regulatory overlay myself, weighting the EMA approval timelines against the commercial launch schedule, factoring in the post-Brexit realignment of—
But what comes out of my mouth is not that.

"Meridian's French subsidiary — Aquilane SA — is running a parallel filing with the ANSM that isn't publicly disclosed. The timeline accelerated in Q3 after they secured the Lyon facility expansion, which gives them manufacturing capacity for the Phase III pipeline product. That's why the European risk is actually understated in the prospectus by about fifteen percent — the regulatory exposure is hedged against a filing that could close by March."
The room goes quiet.
Not contemplative-quiet. Wrong-quiet. The quiet of eleven people simultaneously realizing that something doesn't fit.
I feel it before I understand it. The shift in the air. The way the silence has texture, density. My pulse, which has been steady for twelve minutes, stutters.
Aquilane SA. The ANSM parallel filing. The Lyon facility expansion.

None of that is in the model. None of that is in the prospectus. None of that is in any file on any server at Sterling Capital, because I didn't learn it from Sterling Capital. I learned it in Paris. In a gallery, over wine, from a woman named Celeste who described Meridian's European operations with the casual precision of someone who'd seen the documents firsthand. I learned it and I forgot I learned it and now I've just said it out loud in front of eleven people, and one of those people is—
"Where did you get that data?"
Julian's voice cuts through the silence like a blade through silk. Calm. Too calm. The kind of calm that's a controlled burn, each word placed with surgical precision.
His eyes are on me. Those gray, dissecting eyes, and they are not surprised anymore. They are sharpened to points.

My blood runs cold. Literally — I feel the chill start at my sternum and radiate outward, turning my spine to ice, making my fingers numb against the clicker. The taste in my mouth is adrenaline sour, metallic and sharp, the taste of a body that knows it's in danger before the mind catches up.
"I—" My voice cracks. I swallow. "It was in the supplementary materials. Appendix C."
The lie is so thin I can see through it. There is no Appendix C. The prospectus runs through Appendix B. I know this. I've read the prospectus four times. Julian knows this. He wrote the prospectus.
He doesn't say anything. His eyes hold mine for one beat, two, three — long enough that someone clears their throat, long enough that the silence becomes its own form of violence. Then he looks away.
"Continue," he says.

I continue. The next fifteen minutes are a blur of automatic pilot, my mouth making sounds that correspond to the model while my brain screams at itself in a locked room. When it's over, when the last slide is done and people are filing out with their coffees and their murmured half-comments, I gather my things with hands that won't stop trembling.
I almost make it to the elevator.

The hallway outside the boardroom is empty. The forty-second floor is exclusive — senior leadership only, which means the corridors are wider, the carpet thicker, the silence more expensive. I'm walking fast, heels clicking, focused on the elevator bank at the end of the hall, when a hand presses flat against the wall beside my head.

I stop. I have no choice. His arm is a barrier — not touching me, but bracketing me, caging me against the walnut-paneled wall. Julian's body is inches away. I can feel the heat of him, the space he takes up, the way the air pressure changes when he leans in.

His face is close. Closer than it's ever been. I can see the texture of his skin, the fine grain, the almost imperceptible scar above his left eyebrow. His eyes are gray and they are searching mine with an intensity that makes my lungs forget how to function.

"You just cited information that isn't in any file I've seen." His voice is low. Quiet. Not a whisper — something tighter than a whisper. "Not in our databases, not in the public record. Not in any due diligence room on Wall Street." His jaw tightens. "How?"

He smells like sandalwood and cold air. The exact same scent. The same sandalwood, the same cold air, the same combination that's been haunting my sleep since Paris. It hits me like a physical force — the recognition, the impossible kinship of two men who are the same man and aren't, who share a scent and a jawline and a way of looking at me like I'm a puzzle they haven't solved yet.
I panic.
"I must have misremembered." The words tumble out, desperate and graceless. "I'm sorry. I've been pulling long hours — I probably conflated it with another deal, or — I'll review the supplementary—"
"There is no supplementary."
"— materials, and I'll—"

I stop. Because he's looking at me like that. Like he's reading the spaces between my words, the architecture of my lie, the load-bearing walls I'm trying to hold up with shaking hands. His face is close enough that I can count his eyelashes, and his expression is — not what I expected.
Not suspicion. Not anger. Something more complicated. Something that shifts like light through water as I watch, moving from interrogation to something else. Something that looks almost like—
Interest.

"Misremembered," he repeats. The word hangs between us. He says it slowly, tasting it, rolling it across his tongue the way you'd test an unfamiliar wine. Like he knows it's a lie and he's choosing not to call it — choosing to hold the truth of my deception in his hands without crushing it, without exposing it, without doing anything with it except noting its weight.
His eyes move across my face. Down to my mouth. Back up. The moment stretches like pulled sugar, thin and bright and ready to shatter.
Then he pulls back.

His hand leaves the wall. He straightens his tie — a single, efficient adjustment, like nothing happened, like he didn't just pin me against a wall and breathe my secrets out of me. His expression resets to neutral. The mask slides back into place, perfect and seamless, and the Julian Vance who exists in the world of Sterling Capital returns as though the other one — the one who looked at me with something other than suspicion — was never there at all.
"Don't let it happen again, Chen."
He walks away. His footsteps are measured, unhurried. He doesn't look back. The hallway swallows him, and I'm left against the wall with my heart beating so hard I can hear it in my teeth.

My desk is a wreckage of cold coffee and highlighters. I sink into my chair. My hands are still shaking — I press them flat against the desk to stop them, and they won't stop. The adrenaline is still cycling through me, sour and electric, turning every thought jagged.

Aquilane SA. The ANSM parallel filing. I said it. I said it out loud, in a room full of people whose entire job is to notice what doesn't fit, and Julian noticed, and now Julian knows that I know something I shouldn't know, and his response was to corner me in a hallway and breathe the same cologne as a man in a Paris gallery and say "misremembered" like it was the most interesting word he'd ever heard.
I pull out my phone. Open the Fork app.

The three paths glow on the screen — the branching timelines, the parallel selves, the architecture of my double life. They're all active. All lit. But underneath them, pulsing in a shade of amber I've never seen before, is a new notification:
⚠️ CROSS-TIMELINE INTEGRITY: 87%. DECREASING.

I stare at it. I tap it. Nothing happens — no explanation, no FAQ, no "learn more" link. Just the warning, pulsing like a heartbeat, and the percentage sitting there in its quiet, mathematical certainty.
Eighty-seven percent.

I don't know what it means. I don't know what happens at eighty-six, or seventy, or zero. I don't know if "integrity" refers to the app, or the timelines, or me. But I know — in the way you know that a sound is wrong, that a smell is off, that the taste in your mouth isn't what it should be — that eighty-seven percent is not a number that goes up.

I close the app. I set my phone face-down on the desk. I press my palms against the cold laminate and I breathe, and the fluorescent lights hum their forty-seven-second cycle, and somewhere far away an accordion plays a song I almost remember, and my hands will not stop shaking.
*Novel: "Glitching Through Fate" by Apex Shaw*
